question: A team sets `camunda.client.mode: saas` to pick up Camunda's SaaS preset, but their `application.yml` still contains several legacy or deprecated `camunda.client.*` properties from an earlier upgrade. Per the Modes section, what is the effect?

options.text: Any other set property — including legacy and deprecated ones — overrides the defaults applied by the mode, so the leftover properties can silently change behaviour.
Mode defaults always win over any other property; legacy and deprecated entries are silently ignored.
Spring Boot refuses to start because `mode` is mutually exclusive with any other connectivity property.
Both sets are merged at runtime, with the legacy properties ignored if they are deprecated.

a.text: The note in the Modes section states explicitly: the defaults applied by the modes are overwritten by any other set property, including legacy/deprecated properties — and tells you to check configuration and logs to avoid unwanted override.

b.text: The docs warn for exactly the opposite reason — leftover properties take precedence, which is why you have to audit them.

c.text: The docs do not describe `mode` as mutually exclusive with all other properties; mode coexists with explicit overrides (intended ones like cloud credentials and unintended ones like legacy keys).

d.text: Deprecation does not stop a property from overriding the mode default; the docs warn that legacy/deprecated values still override.

explanation: Modes (`saas` / `self-managed`) ship meaningful defaults, but those defaults sit at the bottom of Spring's property precedence. Any explicit property — even a deprecated one left behind from a previous upgrade — overrides the mode default, which can produce unwanted behaviour. The fix is to clean up the YAML and watch the logs.
